speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Wood shavings? Did you leave off there with wood shavings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We slept on the-- I don't know. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. Yes, it had to be wood shavings.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it time to start?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wood shaving or straw? Before the war, I used straw.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was so sick from eating so much of that bread, that stale bread. You know, I soaked it in coffee and soaked it in the soup that we used to get. I-- can you imagine after hunger, so many years, where I could eat just about anything, picked up from the ground, from the dirt and I ate. I was so sick of food that I remember once I gave way my soup, my ration, and I didn't want anything for it. I couldn't even think about food I was so sick of it. And sure enough, like I said, within a short time, this bread disappeared. He also had a dog, the the camp Kommandant in the camp. And of course, he used to feed him meat and so on. We used to drive that dog crazy, the two or three guys that come over and steal whenever he had food. We used to steal-- the little hut, from his enclosure. Luckily, the dog couldn't jump out. Otherwise, he would've tearing us apart. In that camp, like I said, I was until January. We worked in that-- in the [NON-ENGLISH]. That bunker, that was the hardest we ever had. That was the worst because it had to be finished on a certain date, and we worked on that day and night, carrying that-- pouring that concrete into that-- I think, that's what they said-- the 35-foot thick ceiling with concrete-- iron, whatever. I think a lot of people were killed, you know, in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We also had to carry bricks. When we built these walls around the refineries-- that-- oh god, that was a tough one. Yo know, you carry bricks on a-- it's like an L-shaped board, a long L-shaped board, with two handles that rest on your shoulder, see? And they put these eight to 10 bricks-- I  don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But if you're that skinny that these two bones are sticking out-- when the Poles carried the bricks, I remember before the war, they used to put something, a little pillow underneath. They didn't give us any pillow. It used to cut you in half. And you used to carry those brick up on a scaffolding. It was slippery because the snow, when it melted and then it froze again. And we used to walk on these wooden shoes. And another thing, you never wore the same pair of shoes. They used-- if one shoe broke or was worn out-- I'm talking about the wooden shoes, not leather shoes-- you changed one, and then changed another one. So one was smaller, one was bigger. You were lucky you could walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any particular things about [NON-ENGLISH] that stick out of my mind? The beating that I had, and the-- I used to get from my camp Kommandant all the time. Once they put me-- and I don't remember what I did, probably he just didn't like me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put me-- there was one punishment called [SPEAKING GERMAN] which means bending your knees. You had to squat down on the ground, on your knees, stretched out your arms, and they put the belt across here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how can you keep a belt like that? I mean, you're bent in. Every time I bent in, bang. They hit you. Or they put the stick-- you were lucky if they put the stick on you. And if the stick slipped off, you got beaten with the stick. These were the kind of punishments they used to get.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, one time I  remember the fun. That was New Year's Eve, 1944/'45, 31st of December, 1944. We had a party. We had quite a few entertainers in camp. There was one guy I remember, Hungarian Jewish guy. He was a singer in the Hungarian radio, terrific tenor. We had a few acrobats from somewhere, and jokers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans that worked on the camp, the guards-- I don't remember whether the camp Kommandant was in, but I remember some officers. They were brought into our barracks, and these guys performed. I remember standing near the wall in the back. I always liked to be in the back so that they don't see me because I get beaten enough without being in the front. But I remember thinking, I remember my thoughts. Will I ever get out of here? That was 31st of December, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: And right away at the beginning of January, we heard rumors. We saw, according to the preparations of the Germans-- there's something is happening. And they told us that we were ready to move out. They were going to evacuate us into Germany, to different camps. I don't remember exactly what-- that was either the 16th, or the 17th, or the 15th of January, when we marched out of the camp. When we marched out, I was among the first one, being the tall-- one of the taller ones. And we marched five abreast, the whole column of about 800 or 900 people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The snow was that high. And in our shoes, it was-- wasn't very comfortable. I don't even remember whether we got any food to go. Maybe a piece of bread. I don't know. No soup on the way. Nothing. We marched, I think, a day and a night until we got to Auschwitz. Before the gate, the famous gate, with the 'Arbeit Macht Frei,' or about 500 yards before that on a field, we stopped. That march was murder. Like I said, when we marched out of [NON-ENGLISH], I was among the first, maybe the third or fourth row. When we got to Auschwitz I was already more than 100 yards behind the whole group, me and another couple of guys and two guards watching us with the rifles. We already had a separate guard. Because I was tired, I couldn't walk any further. The snow was that high. And the worst thing, I was so tired. And here, my legs were hurting so bad, I couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: No food. And if you grabbed some snow, you know, if you ate some snow, they hit you over the head with the butt of the rifle. Why? I don't know. What do they care if we die? They were just-- maybe they thought we'd enjoy it too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we stopped, after a while, the camp Kommandant, Hovel, came out and asked, he wants 100 volunteers that cannot run any further. The expression in German is 'laufen.' [SPEAKING GERMAN] laufen. Laufen in German, the literal translation is running, but it also means walking they say. Laufen means walking also. I was the first volunteer. I was the first one to step out. That was the first time in my life, in my camp life, that I volunteered for something. That was because I couldn't walk any further. And I thought-- again using my logic-- that they'll put me the wagons because there were wagons beside us and following us with all the materials for the Germans, the foodstuffs, everything of theirs. The Germans were also walking. And when they changed, they got on the wagons when they changed the guards. I was the first one. Within a few minutes, there were 100 people. When I looked around who the 100 people are, I realized that I made a mistake. Old, broken-down bodies. Old. The oldest was maybe 30. If I was 17 at that time, the oldest couldn't have been more than 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: Old broken-- we called them muzelmen. I don't know whether you ever heard this expression. A muzelman was somebody that's ready to die, you know? Nothing. Just bones.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my friends called to me, come on out! They realized what was happening, that they're going to take us to the crematorium. We were right outside of Auschwitz. I didn't leave for two reasons. First of all, I couldn't get out because they were already-- we were already surrounded by the guards, by different guards. And secondly, I didn't even have the energy to do anything. So I figured, all right. And then I remember my thoughts when they started marching us towards Auschwitz. I remember my thoughts. I was so much at peace. I was completely resigned. I knew that we're going to the crematorium, for the gas chambers. I-- I was 100% sure, but I didn't care. And I thought of myself, well, the whole family's gone anyway, I knew that. But one thought kept me going, a happy thought. I knew that when I get to the gas chamber, I'll be able to sit down. When we get undressed, I'll be able to sit down. I was so tired. You know, these 500 yards took maybe an hour and a half because we couldn't walk. They kept on yelling, [GERMAN]. Hang on in fives. You know, we had to march in fives. We just, we just fell apart. People couldn't walk. I mean, it was like, like leading a hundred corpses. We finally made it through the gate, you know, the 'Albeit Macht Frei' gate into Auschwitz. Out on the field, Auschwitz is a completely different thing. It's not Birkenau. Birkenau had these long barracks. Auschwitz had buildings, two-story brick buildings, beautiful two-story brick building. We come in and we see thousands of people dressed up, and generally looked fairly well dressed, with loaves of bread under the arm, and sausages, pieces of sausage. They got the ration.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we asked them-- we managed to talk to them, but we didn't realize that there aren't that many guards behind us, that we're in there. So we asked them, where are the crematoria? They start like, are you crazy? What crematoria? The crematoria was taken apart a couple of months ago. They were blown up and some were taken apart. So we thought that they're saying that on purpose, you know, not to scare us. And they marched us further into the camp. And they took off 30 people, put them in one barrack-- one of these blocks, then another.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think I was among the last one. They sent us into a building, which was [NON-ENGLISH], by the surgical hospital. I go in there with the other 30 people, I look around. There's nobody behind us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we asked the people there-- I met a few guys that were from Lodz. I didn't know them, but they told me they were from Lodz. And I told them who we were. I said, that we-- they're supposed to take us to the gas chamber. They said, are you crazy? There's nobody here. What gas chambers? Do you see anybody? Do you see any Germans behind you? They didn't take our names. Nothing. And he asked, are you hungry? I said, of course, we're hungry. We didn't eat for two days. So there were these small can, about that high, 25 liters or 20 liters.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, help yourself. That was about half full with soup, thick, frozen, already cold. I put my head in with my hands. I ate. And that was the beginning of the end. I stayed in that hospital until the 27th when we were liberated. And since I was well, I wasn't sick, we tended to the sick people. There were people with broken arms, half the surgeries, open bellies. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we cleaned the toilets, and we, we cooked. We served them the food, but we already had plenty of food then because we grabbed out of the, the magazines that they kept the food.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days before, they managed to send in-- I think it was two days, on the Thursday, the 25th-- a truck or two trucks came in. And they called up, everybody should go down. They're going to take us away. With me in that-- in that hospital was a guy from the Vienna [NON-ENGLISH]. From the soccer team, the first 11, [INAUDIBLE], I remember his name. He had his legs broken eight time when they bombed the camp that he was in. He could hardly walk. And he said to me, let's go down. You gotta go.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, are you crazy? If you go down, you're finished. They're definitely going to shoot us. He said, what's going to go on? They're going to blow up the barracks? I said, all right. So they'll blow them up. You go down there, they'll definitely kill you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason I was that clever was two days after I arrived in Auschwitz, when I was in that hospital hiding there, a few guys came back from our transport. They ran away from the transport, and they had no where else to go. So they came back to Auschwitz, and they hid in the barracks together with us. And they told us, that as soon as they marched out of Auschwitz, together with the rest of the Auschwitz prisoners, in a little town called [GERMAN] That's very funny. [GERMAN] in English means heavenly. In this little town, our camp Kommandant, Kovel, walked around with his pistol. And whoever couldn't walk any further, he just gave them on bullet. Just walked around-- and there were plenty sitting around, just couldn't-- Before-- until Auschwitz, they didn't do it. But as as we left Auschwitz, he just walked around, killed everybody. So the guy said to me, you were very smart because you would've been-- and sure enough, I would've been the first one because I couldn't walk any further.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to him, to [INAUDIBLE], I said, hey. Don't go. If they blow up-- no matter what, we're going to get killed. But if you go down with them, you have no hope. So he didn't go down. We went to bed. I just covered myself as if I was sick. He went to bed. Within a half hour, people started coming back, one after the other. We asked them, what happened? What's happening? Secret. Nobody says anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] They didn't know what happened. Apparently, they got either a phone call or on the radio, they told them to get the hell out of Auschwitz. They're going to blow up the last bridge on the Vistula, which run past the, near Krakow. Forget the prisoners. Get out. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The next day, I went down. They had these warehouses with the food and all different kind of things. Since I was well, we ate. You know, when the Russians came in, we ate-- what was it? I remember. I try to remember that. Macaroni with milk, with butter, with honey, and with marmalade.

speaker: interviewee
text: We cooked all that together because that's all we could find. I mean, If I just looked at it now, I'd probably die. I think that in these 10 days, I put back on all the weight and more, because I think I ate 24 hours a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went down to that warehouse. They told-- this-- we had a guy. He was a [NON-ENGLISH], a ritual slaughterer from Lodz. He was an old man, probably 40 or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was already also in hospital. He was a joker. So he used to cook for us. He said, he needs some margarine. Go down and get us some margarine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went down to the warehouse to get a few package of the margarine. While I'm in the warehouse, I heard a noise out in the yard. And I heard a truck, and I heard German yelling.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I heard a shot, and I heard somebody crying in the next room. So I figured, the Germans came back to load up the trucks again. Said I better hide here. So I hid behind the sacks with the food. And I'm sitting there for an hour. And here, I'm busting. I need to go to the toilet. So I did. And I'm sitting there and sitting. All of a sudden, I hear it's quiet. And I hear movements again. So I figured our guys are-- they came down with them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I walk out. And as I walk out, I nearly died. A guy-- a German, with a totenkopf here, you know? The skull with the crossbones, an SS man, walks right towards me. [GERMAN] What, what do I tell him? So I told him I have a sick brother and they told me to come down and get some sugar, some margarine. So he said to me, get the hell out of here. And he took out his gun. Or maybe I thought he took out his gun. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw him fumbling for the gun. I remembered from movies that I saw before the war, American movies, cowboys and bandits, that if you run away when they shoot after you, you go in a zig-zag. So I started to run like that. He probably had left already. Maybe he figured, ah, the hell with it. Stinking Jew. I won't kill him. And when I came back to the barracks, I tell you, I nearly died from fright. And Saturday, January 27th, 50 years ago today, around 2:00 or 2:30 in the afternoon, we saw the first Russians. And that was the most beautiful sight in the world. The first ones that came in, the first patrol was on skis, covered completely with white sheets so they blended in with the snow. And within an hour, they came in from everywhere. Tore open the gates, the wires. I remember running down the stairs thinking, you better get a hold of your self because you're going to go crazy. Six years, and all of sudden, you're free. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember asking a Russian soldier, what do we do now, because you know, you speak Polish. It's very similar to Russian. He said, what the hell do I care? Just go home. That's it. Or grab a rifle and go beat the hell out of the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Auschwitz till March because I had nothing to go home to. I befriended the guy from Belgium, from Liege. He was an old man. I think 30-something. He was already married before the war. I befriended him very much, and he wanted like, sort of, to adopt me. He said, you come back with me to Belgium. My wife is there. You'll have a home. You got nobody. I just couldn't-- I could leave Poland without going back to Lodz. And I think in March, middle of March, the train started to go normally. I went back to Lodz. And of course, there was nobody there. I just found-- I went-- the first thing you do, you go to the Jewish Committee and you look at the walls. People left names. That's how people found each other. That's how people looked for people. Put your name on a piece of paper and stuck it there. There were no computers to press. And that's how you found people. I didn't find anybody. Nobody. [SIGHS] I found some friends from the ghetto-- I knew from the ghetto. And he took me in. He was a little older, five, six years older than me. He already had an apartment. He took-- the war wasn't over yet. And I stayed with three more guys. I looked for a job. I got a job in the Polish newspaper. I was a runner, you know, going to the printers, back and forth with news items. And one time, I worked in a restaurant washing dishes and helping in the kitchen, an elegant restaurant. Somebody, somebody I know sent me there. And when I befriended these three guys that I lived with, and then I met other people. And I went back to the-- met people from the organization, from before the war, from the bund. At that time, there was still legal. The communists doesn't close them down yet. Went to some meetings. At one time, we got the-- a huge apartment for-- to have the-- our meetings there. It had to be cleaned out. It was full of bricks. I don't know whether it was bombed or something. And the Kommandant of all the camps around Lodz, where they kept the German prisoners, the Volksdeutsche and all the-- send us-- gave us a permit to get a few Germans to clean up the place. They sent me over with a Polish policeman, and we led them through the town. And I was in charge of these German women to clean up the apartment. It was very bizarre for me to be on the other end. And some of my friends from before the war, school friends, decided after a while, things didn't look good in Poland. We didn't know-- it was Russian, the restrictions. There was no work, no food, nothing. It was just like during the war. We were rationing then.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, you know what? We're going to Germany. Want to come with us? I said, why not? I mean, what did I have to do? I didn't have to liquidate any business. I didn't have to tell anybody. I just took my backpack with about four or five things. I didn't have anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got on the train, and we went to Germany. We traveled on top of the train, on the roof. Two nights, from Lodz to Poznan, same city my father was in, which was closer to Germany. From Poznan to Berlin. Hundreds of people sitting on top because there was no room inside. And we came to Berlin. And again, you go to the Jewish Committee. You get food stamps. They register you. And then I went all over Germany looking for family, looking for friends. Of course, I met some friends on the way. We were in Bergen-Belsen, where I met friends. In Feldafing I met friends. In [NON-ENGLISH], I met friends. We traveled for weeks. I was-- that was in July/August, it was still hot. You got a ration in one Jewish committee, one town. I remember got a few rolls, bread rolls, and I got some margarine, a packet of margarine. I wore a jacket with some pockets up here, and I'm putting the margarine in here. We came to a town called [NON-ENGLISH]. This is a sister city to Nuremberg. It's right-- in fact, they call it either [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were tired and hot. We, we got out of the train. We slept under a bridge, right near the train station. I fell asleep. It was hot. And I woke up, and that margarine was all over me. I was so sick of the whole thing. I said to my friend, you know Yashi, I'm not going anywhere. It's enough. I'm not moving out of here. We're going to Jewish Committee. We're going to register here and we're staying here. That's it I'm not traveling any further. It's driving me crazy. I'm not finding anybody. It was already six months-- well, the war ended in May, but it was already about six or seven months since we were liberated, wouldn't hear anything. I went to the  Jewish Committee and filled out a register. And they, right away, assigned us to a private apartment, to a private family, German family, for lodging. Gave us two separate rooms. I got one room, my friend got a  room. They fed us. They were very nice to us. They fed us, like, cooked for us. Everything. And we came back to the Jewish Committee and there was a sign there saying that Major Milton [? Peskin ?] of the United States Air Force, R on the airfield, R-- I even remember the name of the airfield-- R28, is asking any refugee survivors of the concentration camps that want to work to come out and see him out at the airport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not? So we hitch a ride with the Americans, American jeep went past. In fact, there were some officers. He opened the door. I walked in and left him outside. It was very funny. I left an American officer standing outside, so he pushed, and pushed me over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us out to the airport. And we come in. The major was interviewing us. He can't speak Yiddish. He can't speak German. He speaks English. I speak Yiddish. I called an interpreter to translate from German to English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he asked me, you want to work? Yes, I said. Of course. He said-- the only thing I wanted to work is near a kitchen where there's food. I didn't care about anything else. He said to me, what did you do before the war? I went to school. He said, what did you do in concentration camp? And again, I said, well, I was carrying bricks. He said, well, we can't let you carry bricks here. So I sort of-- I didn't want to just right away tell him, hey, listen. I want to be-- go to the kitchen. So I said to him, if you have any work in the kitchen, I could work. He looked at me like I was crazy. Work in the-- who wants to work in the kitchen? He said, if you want to work in the kitchen, OK. So he called in some of the officers that were in charge of the kitchen, and he sent us in there. And there were a couple of guys sitting, Jewish kids also out of the concentration camp. A friend-- in fact, a very close friend of mine that lives here in LA-- last week, we're celebrating his 70th birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to work in the American-- in the Air Force kitchen. And we had a ball there. They treated us like royalty. This is the uniform that you see, where they gave us uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then after a while they said we have to dye them. Navy didn't let us wear the green uniform. But who cared? We wore them anyway. We had everything, cigarettes, everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned to smoke. I mean, it wasn't fashionable not to. I nearly choked to death learning to smoke, but I learned. We had everything, food clothing. We lived in that apartment in town, in Furth. I worked there for quite a few months. That was, say, in September. I worked through September, October, November. December-- I don't know. Some friends sent me-- I don't know why-- sent me to Feldafing, which was south. It was near Munich, to a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.
